This error message means that none of the available functions for the `TextField` component can be called with the arguments provided in the code.

To fix this error, you need to review the arguments passed to the `TextField` component and ensure they match one of the available function signatures.

For example, if you are passing a `String` as the first argument, you should use the following function signature:

```
TextField(
    value = yourString, 
    onValueChange = { yourString = it },
    // other arguments...
)
```

If you are passing a `TextFieldValue` object as the first argument, you should use the following function signature:

```
TextField(
    value = yourTextFieldValue, 
    onValueChange = { yourTextFieldValue = it },
    // other arguments...
)
```

If you are still unsure what is causing the error or how to fix it, check the documentation for the `TextField` component or review some sample code to see how it is used.

when the textField value is null, it will show 0, how to modify the code so that it shows nothing

You can modify the `value` parameter of the `TextField` component to check if `stock` is null `(stock == null)`, and if so, pass an empty string `("")` as the value. Here's how you can modify your code to achieve this:

```
TextField(
    value = stock?.toString() ?: "",
    onValueChange = { newText ->
        stock = newText.toIntOrNull()
    },
    supportingText = { Text("Stock") },
    keyboardOptions = KeyboardOptions(
        keyboardType = KeyboardType.Number,
        imeAction = ImeAction.Done
    ),
    keyboardActions = KeyboardActions(onDone = { })
)
```

In this code, the `value` parameter uses the elvis operator `(stock?.toString() ?: "")` to check if `stock` is null. If `stock` is null, it returns an empty string `""`. If `stock` is not null, it returns `stock.toString()`.

Also, note that the `onValueChange` function assigns `newText.toIntOrNull()` directly to `stock` without checking if it's not `null`, since `null` is a possible value for `toIntOrNull()`.
are there a equivalent to upsert in firestore?

Yes, there is an equivalent to `upsert` in Firestore called `set` with the option of `merge`. 

When you use the `set` method with the `merge` option, Firestore will update the document only with the fields that are specified in the data, without overwriting the entire document. If the document does not exist, then Firestore will create a new document with the specified fields.

Here's an example of how to use `set` with the `merge` option:

```
// Create a reference to a document
val docRef = db.collection("cities").document("LA")

// Set the data and merge it with existing fields if document exists
val data = hashMapOf(
    "name" to "Los Angeles",
    "state" to "CA",
    "country" to "USA",
    "population" to 4000000
)

docRef.set(data, SetOptions.merge())
        .addOnSuccessListener { Log.d(TAG, "Document updated successfully!") }
        .addOnFailureListener { e -> Log.w(TAG, "Error updating document", e) }
```

In this code, `SetOptions.merge()` is applied to the `set()` method to merge the specified data with the existing fields in the document. If the document does not exist, a new one will be created with the specified fields.

You can use the `document()` method to create a reference to the document that you want to update. Then, you can use the `set()` method with the `merge` option to update the document with only the fields that have changed:

```
firestore
    .collection("expenses/Yearly/Monthly/monthly_expenses/Weekly/weekly_expenses/Daily/daily_expenses")
    .document(item.id) // Get reference to the document
    .set(updatedItem, SetOptions.merge()) // Update the document with only fields that have changed
    .addOnSuccessListener { Log.d(TAG, "Document updated successfully!") }
    .addOnFailureListener { e -> Log.w(TAG, "Error updating document", e) }
```

In this code, the `document()` method is called with `item.id` to create a reference to the document that you want to update. Then, `set()` is called with `updatedItem` and `SetOptions.merge()` to update the document with only the fields that have changed.

Note that you may need to adjust the document path and/or the reference key depending on the structure of your Firestore database.

The error message "Illegal pattern character 'e'" occurs because the string `"Week_" + weekNum + "_MMM_yyyy"` supplied to `SimpleDateFormat` contains an illegal character 'e' in the pattern.

The letter 'e' is not a valid pattern character for date formatting. You may have meant to use a different character instead, or you may have mistyped the pattern string.

To fix this, you can replace the 'e' character with a valid pattern character or remove it altogether if it is not needed. For example, if you wanted to add a day of the week as well, you could use the pattern `"EEE, MMM d, ''yy"` which would output a date like "Thu, Jan 14, '22". If you only need to output the week number and year, you could use the pattern `"w/yyyy"`.

Here's an example of how to use the revised patterns:

```
fun currentWeek(): String {
    val weekNum = currentDate[Calendar.WEEK_OF_YEAR]
    val year = currentDate[Calendar.YEAR]
    val dateFormat = SimpleDateFormat("w/yyyy", Locale.getDefault())
    return "Week_" + dateFormat.format(currentDate.time)
}
```

This code will format the current date to output the week number and year in the pattern "w/yyyy", which can be prefixed with "Week_" if necessary. You can change the pattern to fit your specific needs.

the problem I'm facing is for example I've added a new item with name = ABC, and I from the app I input a new name which is CBA, It will replace the current name field, whats the solution?

If you only want to update certain fields of a Firestore document without overwriting the entire document, you can use the `update()` method instead of `set()` as follows:

```
firestore.collection("your_collection").document("your_document_id")
    .update(mapOf(
        "name" to updatedName,
        // Any other fields to be updated with their new values
    ))
```

The `update()` method allows you to specify a map of field-value pairs that will be updated in the Firestore document. Only the fields that are listed in the update map will be updated, leaving the other fields untouched. If a field that does not exist in the document is added to the update map, it will be created.

Using the `update()` method will allow you to modify only the specified field values while keeping the other fields of the document intact. Keep in mind that if you are updating a field that does not yet exist in the document, the new field will be created.
for example in one day which is firestore document, I have 3 fields with many values

You can update specific fields in a Firestore document that has multiple fields using dot notation to specify the nested field path in the update map.

For example, if you have a Firestore document representing a day and you want to update the values of three fields within that document, you can use the `update()` method with a map that specifies the nested fields you want to update like this:

```
val dailyExpensesRef = firestore
    .collection("expenses")
    .document("Yearly/Monthly/monthly_expenses/Weekly/weekly_expenses/Daily/daily_expenses")

dailyExpensesRef.update(mapOf(
    "field1.value1" to updatedValue1,
    "field2.value2" to updatedValue2,
    "field3.value3" to updatedValue3
)).addOnSuccessListener {
    Log.d(TAG, "Document successfully updated!")
}.addOnFailureListener {
    Log.e(TAG, "Error updating document", it)
}
```

In this example code, `dailyExpensesRef` is a reference to the Firestore document representing a day's expenses. The `update()` method is used to update the values of three fields within the document: "field1.value1", "field2.value2", and "field3.value3". These field paths should match the structure of your Firestore document.

Using dot notation allows you to specify the exact nested field you want to update in the document without affecting the other fields in the document.
